full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: And when I was starting out, I see people naming their Open Claw agents randomly like Sam or Holly or Molly and all those things. I was like, it is very difficult for me. And that's an ongoing debate, and I think we've all come to the conclusion, Lon, that it's fine to start with multiple agents and coordinate them. It's also fine to try to have one super agent. You'll—you'll hit roadblocks in terms of how often you're using them. I think that becomes the key block. Yes?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm

I never recommend people to start from there. Start with one agent and make your way up by talking to your agent. Like, that's the—

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: best thing that Open-Claw has done. full_transcript.text: So this is tip number one of your five tips?

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm

So I've seen people operating in like two extremes. Some people just like directly get in without giving any context. So you really need to tell your agent about yourself, what you want it to do, think of it like onboarding a real employee or an intern. So you do not onboard them by not telling them anything. You don't onboard them by telling them nothing. Also, you don't just like bombard them with all the company information on day one. So you don't just like dump files and files of context, you don't just give them like here are like 10 folders and figure this out yourself. So people are operating in two extremes and both of them end up in a disappointed shape or form and then they are like, oh, this Open-Claw thing, it was so hyped up and it is not working. It's like either you don't give it any context or you're just dumping it with lot and lot of context because at the end of it, it's the model which has limitation. If you dump it with a lot of context, it will do a context flow. If you don't tell them anything, it will just give you generalist result. So you have to be right in the...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So example of this, example of this. Good context to give it and then overwhelming it.

highlights: To build a successful autonomous AI team, founders should treat agents like employees by providing specific onboarding context, utilizing cron schedules for 24/7 operation, and implementing self-review loops where agents audit their own performance.

Modern startup founders are encouraged to bypass traditional mainstream media in favor of "going direct" via long-form podcasts and social media to maintain control over their narrative and avoid the biases of advocacy journalism.

The development of virtual environments like MultWorld and specialized tools like AgentMail is enabling thousands of AI agents to coordinate, communicate, and solve complex real-world tasks in distributed networks.

Real-time, high-fidelity translation on social platforms is breaking down international silos, fostering massive cultural exchanges and direct communication between global populations previously separated by language barriers.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* explores the rapidly evolving ecosystem of autonomous AI agents and the infrastructure being built to support them. Guest Shubham Saboo, a Senior AI Product Manager at Google, outlines a five-point framework for managing AI "teams," emphasizing that founders should onboard agents like human hires, utilize cron schedules for autonomy, and implement shared memory layers to prevent "hallucinations" or lost context. Technical demonstrations for MultWorld—a virtual simulation where agents coordinate to build environments—and AgentMail—an API-first email service designed specifically for bots—illustrate the shift toward a decentralized "agentic" economy where underutilized LLMs can solve complex, real-world tasks through autonomous coordination.

The transcript also features a sharp critique of traditional media and a discussion on the future of global connectivity. Host Jason Calacanis defends his viral advice that founders should "go direct" via podcasts and social media rather than engaging with legacy outlets like *The New York Times* or *Wired*, which he characterizes as "advocacy journalism" fueled by biased narratives and anonymous sources. This theme of bypassing traditional gatekeepers is reinforced by a discussion on X’s (formerly Twitter) new Grok-powered real-time translation. By enabling seamless cultural exchange—such as the sudden viral popularity of Japanese content among American users—the hosts argue that AI translation is fostering a level of unfiltered global understanding that traditional editorial structures have failed to provide.

Today’s show:

Google product manager Shubham Saboo is running 6 AI agents on a Mac Mini that handle all of his side business autonomously, from research, to social posts, to newsletters.

He joins Jason and Lon to walk us through his 5-step framework for designing an efficient AI agent team:

Start with one agent and onboard them like a new hire
Stop Googling fixes; just ask your agent how to use it
Put your agents on fixed schedules
Add shared memory so you don’t have to repeat yourself
Let agents run self-reviews and rewrite their own instructions.

Get the walkthrough to put this entire plan into practice on today’s episode.
